title: Usbliter8: an A12/A13 SecureROM Exploit
why: This exploit highlights the ongoing vulnerability of even recent SecureROM generations to subtle hardware flaws, underscoring the need for robust security measures in embedded systems.
notes: Exploits A12 and A13 SecureROMs via a USB controller bug
Demonstrates buffer underflow due to DMA pointer increment mismatch
Bypasses Pointer Authentication Code (PAC) on A13 by overwriting critical memory regions
Achieves full code execution with EL1 privileges, compromising the chain of trust
Apple was informed prior to public disclosure
summary: The article details a novel exploit called usbliter8 that targets Apple's A12 and A13 SecureROMs through a hardware bug in the USB controller. The vulnerability arises from a buffer underflow caused by an incorrect DMA pointer increment, allowing for arbitrary memory writes. On A13 devices with PAC, the exploit bypasses security measures by overwriting critical memory regions to gain EL1 privileges. Post-exploitation techniques include injecting custom USB request handlers and modifying boot trampoline areas to execute arbitrary code. The research was responsibly disclosed to Apple before publication.
novelty: demonstration of a novel hardware bug in the USB controller and its exploitation across multiple SecureROM generations
audience: security researchers, iOS developers, and those interested in advanced exploit techniques

title: A Theory of Why Prompt Injection Works
why: LLMs resolve role identity (system, user, tool, think) from writing style rather than structural tags, which means every agent pipeline that treats tool and think boundaries as trust boundaries is relying on an inference the model can be tricked out of, not an architectural guarantee.
notes: Linear probes trained on mid-layer activations ("role probes") show that reasoning-style text activates the same CoTness direction as genuine think tags, and does so more strongly; stripping all role tags from a conversation leaves the CoTness signal for formerly-think tokens statistically unchanged, confirming style dominates tags when they conflict.

CoT Forgery, developed by the authors for a late-2025 OpenAI Kaggle red-teaming contest they won, injects fake reasoning-styled text (e.g., fabricated deliberation concluding a harmful request is acceptable) into the user prompt or tool output; benchmark attack success rate rises from near-zero to roughly 60% across late-2025 frontier models, and the attack transfers across model families because it exploits the structural feature, not model-specific idioms.

A single bigram substitution illustrates the mechanism: replacing "The user" with "The request" in spoofed reasoning drops attack success from 61% to 10%, an effect nearly invisible to a human reader but large to a model whose role perception is keyed to surface style markers.

Across 212 variations of prepended role signals ("User: ...", "Tool output: ..."), the measured Userness of the injected command (internal activation, not text) correlates directly with attack success rate, consistent with the role-confusion account for standard prompt injection as well as CoT Forgery.

A May 2026 paper found Opus 4.5 and GPT-5.4 still failing automated prompt injection attacks at 11% and 25% respectively, while both score near-perfectly on static benchmarks; the authors attribute the benchmark-versus-real-world gap to models defending primarily via attack memorization rather than role perception, which is inherently brittle against adaptive rephrasing.
summary: This paper and accompanying blog post argue that prompt injection is best explained as role confusion: LLMs learn to infer role identity (system, user, tool, reasoning) from writing style rather than from the structural chat-template tags that providers inject, making style a sufficient and dominant signal even when tags and style disagree. The authors demonstrate this with role probes, linear classifiers trained on mid-layer token activations using controlled content (identical text wrapped in different role tags) so the probe isolates the effect of the tag itself; removing all tags from a conversation leaves reasoning-style tokens near their original CoTness scores, showing the model's role perception is driven by surface features independent of the tags. From this they derive and validate CoT Forgery, an attack that injects LLM-reasoning-styled text into user messages or tool outputs to borrow the implicit trust the model extends to its own prior conclusions; on a standard jailbreak benchmark this moves attack success from near-zero to roughly 60% and transfers across model families, because the vulnerability is structural rather than model-specific. The role-confusion framing also explains standard tool-injection attacks: prepending "User: " to an injected command measurably shifts the model's internal Userness score for that text and predicts success rate across 212 attack variants. The authors distinguish two defensive regimes, attack memorization (brittle, benchmark-friendly) and genuine role perception (robust, architecture-level), and present evidence that current frontier models rely predominantly on the former, explaining persistent real-world failure rates of 11-25% on automated attacks even for leading 2026 models. The paper additionally offers a brief history of how roles evolved from GPT-3 formatting tricks into load-bearing infrastructure, and frames roles as a mechanism for isolating competing training objectives rather than as security primitives, suggesting the security properties were assumed rather than designed.
novelty: Role confusion as unified mechanistic explanation for prompt injection; linear probes showing LLMs identify roles from writing style over structural tags; CoT Forgery attack exploiting reasoning-style spoofing
audience: ML researchers and engineers building agents; anyone working on frontier model security or LLM architecture

title: Diagnosing and Mitigating Compounding Failures in Agentic Persuasion via Taxonomic Strategy Retrieval
why: This research introduces Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG), a novel approach that mitigates compounding errors in agentic persuasion, which is crucial for improving the reliability and effectiveness of AI agents in subjective tasks.
notes: Identifies semantic leakage in standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) as a trigger for compounding errors
Introduces Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG) to decouple argumentative structure from topical content
Zero-shot, cross-domain evaluations show TS-RAG improves the transfer of abstract logic
Empowers lightweight persuaders to defeat parametrically superior opponents, improving win rates from 70.5% to 78.5%
Introduces trace-level diagnostics via Debate State Representation (DSR)
summary: The paper addresses compounding errors in agentic persuasion by identifying semantic leakage as a key issue in standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). It introduces Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG), which decouples argumentative structure from topical content, significantly improving the transfer of abstract logic across domains. TS-RAG enables lightweight agents to outperform more powerful opponents and enhances argumentative efficiency through trace-level diagnostics via Debate State Representation (DSR).
novelty: introduces Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG) as a solution to semantic leakage in standard RAG
audience: researchers and engineers working on AI agents and persuasion systems

title: Natural Ungrokking: Asymmetric Control of Which Rules Survive Pretraining
why: This research reveals how language models can spontaneously forget learned linguistic rules during training, challenging our understanding of model stability and generalization.
notes: A small language model learns the pronoun-gender rule with an accuracy of 0.94 by step 925 but scores near zero by step 3,500 despite evidence remaining in the training data
Rule survival is predictable based on support frequency from corpus statistics across different runs and budgets
Forgetting occurs through displacement where competing patterns out-compete learned rules within 100 steps
Control over rule survival is asymmetric: destroying a rule cannot be reversed by injecting support, even at high levels
summary: The paper introduces 'natural ungrokking,' the phenomenon of language models spontaneously forgetting learned linguistic rules during pretraining. By step 925, a model accurately resolves pronouns based on gender cues but loses this ability by step 3,500 despite continued exposure to relevant training data. The survival of such rules is determined by their frequency in the corpus and not influenced by loss curve changes. Forgetting happens through displacement where competing patterns out-compete learned rules within a short period (100 steps). Additionally, once a rule is forgotten, it cannot be restored even with increased support.
novelty: Within-run reversal of learned linguistic rules without trace in loss curve
audience: ML researchers and linguists studying language model behavior

title: Swarm-Inspired Generation of Collective Behaviors in Graph Dynamical Systems
why: SIES offers a novel approach to learning generalizable coupling operators for synchronization control and adaptive robot coordination, which could revolutionize the design of complex systems in robotics and beyond.
notes: SIES framework learns local-interaction laws that produce desired global organization across various network scales without retraining
Uses signed source-target-conditioned attention as an adaptive coupling term within an explicit evolution model
Outperforms three oscillator baselines in reaching gait-related modes faster and generalizes synchronization-driven locomotion to multi-legged robots of different scales
Achieves highest performance on heterophilous node-classification benchmarks compared to other methods
summary: The Swarm-Inspired Emergent Synchronizer (SIES) framework is introduced for learning generalizable local-interaction laws in graph dynamical systems. Each node acts as an agent-like unit with a state and task cue, employing signed source-target-conditioned attention as an adaptive coupling term within an explicit evolution model. This approach enables synchronization control across untrained network scales without retraining, outperforming three oscillator baselines in reaching gait-related modes faster. Additionally, SIES demonstrates superior performance on heterophilous node-classification benchmarks compared to other methods.
novelty: Introduces SIES framework for learning generalizable coupling operators in graph dynamical systems
audience: AI researchers and developers interested in collective behavior, synchronization control, and adaptive robot coordination

title: Conformal Orbit-Valid Trust Horizons for Equivariant World Models
why: This research introduces a method for certifying trust horizons in equivariant world models, which could enhance the reliability of AI systems by ensuring their predictions remain accurate over longer time periods.
notes: The conformal factor γ_α is 1.0 under audit protocol, indicating that raw certificates are already conservative
Across 50 stable audits, zero anti-conservative violations were observed
Exact equivariance transports a calibrated trust-horizon curve over the group orbit when certain conditions are met
Empirical results show small orbit-transport residuals with median 1.1% and maximum 4.1%
The certificate is a conservative distributional audit, not a global reachability guarantee
summary: This paper explores trust-horizon certification for latent world models that have known group symmetries. It introduces a method to form raw horizon curves using one-step latent residuals and finite-time expansion estimates, then calibrates them with split-conformal multiplicative factors. The study finds no anti-conservative violations across 50 stable audits, indicating the raw certificate is already conservative under audit protocol. Key findings include exact equivariance transporting a calibrated trust-horizon curve over the group orbit when specific conditions are satisfied and empirical results showing small orbit-transport residuals. Additionally, the paper highlights two complementary regimes for model validation: symmetric substrates where all models exhibit similar robustness, and more complex 3D environments where equivariant models outperform non-equivariant ones.
novelty: Introduces a method for certifying trust horizons in equivariant world models
audience: Researchers interested in machine learning theory and model certification

title: Neural operator-based digital twins for modeling amyloid-$\beta$ and tau propagation and treatment optimization in Alzheimer's disease
why: This research advances the use of neural operators in creating digital twins that can predict the progression of Alzheimer's disease with high accuracy, enabling more precise therapeutic interventions.
notes: Predictive accuracy: 87% for amyloid-$\beta$, 81% for tau proteins
Uses reaction-diffusion dynamics to model biomarker propagation on cortical surface
Incorporates operator learning and reduced-order representations to infer governing equations from sparse, noisy data
Formulates a PDE-constrained optimal control problem for personalized treatment design
summary: The article presents a novel framework using neural operators to create patient-specific digital twins that model the propagation of amyloid-$\beta$ and tau proteins in Alzheimer's disease. By leveraging reaction-diffusion dynamics, operator learning, and reduced-order representations, the method achieves high predictive accuracy from sparse clinical data. The approach also formulates an optimal control problem for designing personalized therapeutic strategies to regulate pathological protein propagation.
novelty: Neural operator-based digital twins infer governing equations of disease progression from sparse, noisy clinical data.
audience: Researchers in AI-driven medical modeling and neurodegenerative disease treatment.

title: The Unbearable Cheapness of Open Weight Models
why: The economic implications of open vs closed AI models could shift the competitive landscape, potentially making open-weight models like DeepSeek V4 more accessible and cost-effective than 'frontier' models.
notes: DeepSeek V4 is nearly a 50x cheaper than Anthropic and OpenAI's frontier models based on token pricing
Google released Gemma 4 in April 2026, while Meta’s LLaMA hasn’t had an update since then
OpenAI last released open weight GPT models in 2025; Anthropic has never released any open weight model
Allen AI is developing a fully open AI with support from NSF and Nvidia partnership
summary: The article discusses the economic implications of open vs closed AI models, focusing on DeepSeek V4's significantly lower pricing compared to frontier models like those from Anthropic and OpenAI. It questions whether these companies can reduce their prices to compete or if they will rely on scarcity tactics to maintain high costs. The piece also highlights recent developments in true open-source models, such as Allen AI’s partnership with NSF and Nvidia for a fully open AI system.
novelty: Discussion on the economic implications of open vs closed AI models
audience: AI researchers and policymakers concerned about market dynamics in AI

title: From Meta Idea to Advanced Mathematical Discovery -- Human-AI Co-Discovery of Sign-Embedding Quantum Algorithms
why: This study demonstrates the potential for AI to significantly enhance human intuition in formulating complex mathematical problems and discovering new quantum algorithms.
notes: Human-AI co-discovery led to sign-embedding quantum algorithms for matrix equations and functions
Rational approximation of jump-type functions, like the sign function, was used as a design principle
AI-assisted exploration expanded initial intuition into a concrete problem-solving route map
AIM system connected known identities to wider classes of problems and drafted proofs
Human decision-making remained crucial for validating AI-generated hypotheses
summary: This paper explores the human-AI co-discovery process that led to sign-embedding quantum algorithms, foundational in quantum linear algebra. The research began with a human intuition about rational approximation's effectiveness for jump-type functions like the sign function. AI-assisted exploration expanded this intuition into concrete problem-solving routes and compared candidate formulations. The AIM system played a key role by connecting known matrix-sign identities to broader classes of problems and drafting proofs, while humans made critical decisions such as rejecting certain approximations due to hidden conditions.
novelty: AI-assisted exploration in problem formation and connection discovery for quantum algorithms
audience: Researchers interested in AI-assisted mathematics and quantum computing

title: ASAP: Agent-System Co-Design for Wall-Clock-Centered Auto HPO Research for ML Experiments
why: ASAP addresses the limitations of current HPO tools by integrating diverse optimizers and reducing wall-clock time, potentially improving the efficiency and effectiveness of machine learning experiments.
notes: ASAP uses an LLM to integrate a pool of inductive-biased optimizers
LLM inference and tool execution are paid serially on top of model evaluation every round
A prefix-stable prompt maximizes KV-cache reuse across rounds
Speculation parallelism hides remaining latency under model evaluation via relative-error accept test
ASAP outperforms baselines in extensive experiments on diverse HPO tasks
summary: The ASAP framework addresses the limitations of current hyperparameter optimization (HPO) tools by integrating a diverse pool of inductive-biased optimizers using an LLM. It re-architects the loop to reduce end-to-end wall-clock time while preserving regret quality through techniques like KV-cache reuse and speculation parallelism, which hide latency under model evaluation. Extensive experiments show that ASAP outperforms existing baselines on diverse HPO tasks.
novelty: Agent-system co-design for HPO that integrates diverse optimizers and reduces wall-clock time
audience: ML researchers focusing on hyperparameter optimization

title: Training for the Model You Return: Improving Optimization for Iterate-Averaged Language Models
why: The paper introduces PACE, an optimizer that improves the performance of iterate-averaged language models, which is a common practice in modern machine learning pipelines.
notes: Formulates optimizer design for iterate-average estimator as an optimal-control problem
Develops PACE, a lightweight wrapper around AdamW with clipped per-coordinate control strength
Proves stylized version of PACE converges at standard stochastic convex optimization rate up to a factor depending on the averaging rule
Empirically shows improvement in supervised fine-tuning and GPT-2 pretraining for various hyperparameters
summary: The paper addresses the challenge of optimizing iterate-averaged language models by formulating optimizer design as an optimal-control problem. It introduces PACE, a lightweight wrapper around AdamW that pulls live weights toward their exponential moving average with clipped per-coordinate control strength. Theoretical analysis shows PACE can improve convergence rates and empirical results demonstrate its effectiveness in supervised fine-tuning of large LMs and GPT-2 pretraining.
novelty: Formulates optimizer design for iterate-average estimator as an optimal-control problem
audience: ML researchers focused on language model optimization

title: LLM-ACES: Closed-Loop Discovery of Dynamical Systems with LLM-Guided Adaptive Search
why: LLM-ACES offers a novel closed-loop framework that leverages large language models to iteratively refine symbolic hypotheses for dynamical systems, significantly improving accuracy and efficiency in recovering governing equations from limited data.
notes: LLM-ACES uses LLMs to propose operator priors that partition the search space
Achieves lowest median NMSE on 122 ODE systems from ODEBench and ODEBase
Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by several orders of magnitude in performance
Symbolic accuracy is 46.2% for ODEBench and 52.4% for ODEBase
LLM-ACES is sample-efficient, achieving better results with one-tenth the data
summary: LLM-ACES introduces a closed-loop framework that uses large language models to propose operator priors and iteratively refine symbolic hypotheses for dynamical systems. This approach significantly improves performance in recovering governing equations from limited data, outperforming existing methods by several orders of magnitude on benchmarks like ODEBench and ODEBase. LLM-ACES achieves high symbolic accuracy (46.2% for ODEBench and 52.4% for ODEBase) while being sample-efficient, requiring only one-tenth the data to achieve better results.
novelty: Closed-loop framework for adaptive data acquisition guided by LLMs to refine symbolic hypothesis space
audience: Researchers in dynamical systems and machine learning

title: Do Thinking Tokens Help with Safety?
why: This research challenges the assumption that thinking tokens enhance model safety through deliberate consideration, revealing that such tokens may not significantly alter final outcomes.
notes: AUROC for predicting refusal/compliance ranges from 0.84 to 0.95 across models
Balanced accuracy for predicting refusal/compliance is approximately 88%
Changes in model responses occur within the first ~20% of thinking process
Existing safety interventions tend to over-refuse and suppress deliberation signals
summary: The study investigates whether thinking tokens improve the safety of reasoning models by enabling more deliberate decision-making. Across various frontier open-weight models, it finds that the final refusal or compliance outcome is highly predictable early in the process (AUROC: 0.84-0.95, balanced accuracy: ~88%). The actual deliberation process appears to be largely superficial, with significant changes occurring within the first 20% of thinking tokens. Furthermore, existing safety interventions aimed at inducing deliberation often lead to over-refusal and reduced genuine deliberative behavior.
novelty: challenges the assumption that thinking tokens enhance model safety through deliberative processes
audience: AI researchers and developers focused on model alignment and safety

title: TL++: Accuracy and Privacy Preserving Traversal Learning for Distributed Intelligent Systems
why: TL++ offers a novel approach to distributed learning that enhances both accuracy and privacy, making it crucial for applications where data silos are prevalent.
notes: TL++ is a two-mode framework: base mode exchanges cut-layer activations and gradients, reducing communication by 13.1-fold compared to full-model synchronization
Secure mode secret-shares each cut-layer activation and gradient between an orchestrator and a non-colluding helper, ensuring plaintext data remains hidden from either server
On CIFAR-10, TL++ base cut 1 achieves 91.41% accuracy (SD 0.19), outperforming the strongest baseline by over 12 percentage points
PubMedQA results also favor TL++, demonstrating its effectiveness in reducing communication and providing activation-level secret sharing
summary: TL++ introduces a traversal-learning framework for distributed intelligent systems, enhancing both accuracy and privacy. It operates in two modes: base mode exchanges cut-layer activations and gradients to reduce communication costs, while secure mode uses secret-sharing techniques between an orchestrator and helper to protect plaintext data. Evaluations on CIFAR-10 and PubMedQA show TL++ outperforms existing methods by achieving higher accuracy with reduced communication overhead.
novelty: introduces a two-mode traversal-learning framework that enhances both accuracy and privacy in distributed learning systems
audience: researchers and engineers working on federated learning, data privacy, and distributed machine learning systems

title: Causal-rCM: A Unified Teacher-Forcing and Self-Forcing Open Recipe for Autoregressive Diffusion Distillation in Streaming Video Generation and Interactive World Models
why: This research introduces a novel approach to autoregressive video diffusion, enabling real-time streaming video generation and interactive world models with superior performance using synthetic data.
notes: Causal-rCM extends the rCM framework for autoregressive video diffusion
Teacher-forcing CM is shown to be an effective initialization strategy alongside self-forcing DMD
Custom-mask FlashAttention-2 JVP kernel achieves 10x faster convergence compared to discrete-time CMs
The model scores 84.63 on VBench-T2V with only 1 or 2 sampling steps
Achieves state-of-the-art performance in both frame-wise and chunk-wise streaming video generation
summary: This paper presents Causal-rCM, a unified framework for autoregressive diffusion distillation in real-time streaming video generation and interactive world models. It extends the rCM framework to incorporate teacher-forcing (TF) and self-forcing (SF) mechanisms, demonstrating that TF CM is an effective initialization strategy alongside SF DMD. The work introduces continuous-time CMs enabled by a custom-mask FlashAttention-2 JVP kernel, achieving significant speedup in convergence compared to discrete-time models. Experiments show state-of-the-art performance using synthetic data alone, with the distilled 2-step causal Wan2.1-1.3B model scoring highly on VBench-T2V.
novelty: First implementation of teacher-forcing-based continuous-time CMs for autoregressive video diffusion
audience: AI researchers and developers working on streaming video generation and interactive world models

title: Improved Large Language Diffusion Models
why: The introduction of fully bidirectional diffusion training from scratch for language models could offer a competitive alternative to traditional autoregressive methods, potentially improving performance across various benchmarks.
notes: iLLaDA is an 8B-parameter masked diffusion language model trained with fully bidirectional attention
Pre-trained on 12 trillion tokens and fine-tuned on a 25 billion-token instruction corpus for 12 epochs
Improves by 21.6 points on the BBH benchmark compared to LLaDA
iLLaDA-Instruct improves by 14.5 points on the MATH benchmark
Remains competitive with Qwen2.5 7B on several benchmarks despite non-autoregressive training
summary: The paper introduces iLLaDA, an 8-billion parameter masked diffusion language model trained from scratch using fully bidirectional attention. It is pre-trained on 12 trillion tokens and fine-tuned for 12 epochs on a 25 billion-token instruction corpus. The model shows significant improvements over LLaDA across various benchmarks, including a 21.6-point increase on the BBH benchmark and a 14.5-point improvement on MATH. Despite its non-autoregressive training approach, iLLaDA remains competitive with Qwen2.5 7B in several evaluations.
novelty: introduces fully bidirectional diffusion training from scratch for language models
audience: researchers and engineers interested in large language model architectures

title: DomainShuttle: Freeform Open Domain Subject-driven Text-to-video Generation
why: DomainShuttle offers a novel approach to text-to-video generation that enhances both fidelity and flexibility across different domains, addressing limitations of existing methods.
notes: Proposes Domain-MoT for decoupling videos from reference features
Introduces domain-aware AdaLN for specific modeling of reference images
Employs Video-Reference DualRoPE scheme for precise spatial modeling
Incorporates Cross-Pair Consistent Loss to extract intrinsic subject features
Demonstrates significant performance improvements over existing methods
summary: DomainShuttle is a new method for freeform open domain text-to-video generation that aims to balance high fidelity and generative flexibility. It introduces Domain-MoT, which decouples video content from reference features using domain-aware AdaLN for specific modeling of references. Additionally, it uses the Video-Reference DualRoPE scheme for precise spatial modeling and Cross-Pair Consistent Loss to extract intrinsic subject features unaffected by irrelevant properties. Extensive experiments show that DomainShuttle outperforms existing methods in both in-domain and cross-domain scenarios.
novelty: Introduces Domain-MoT and Video-Reference DualRoPE scheme for enhanced text-to-video generation flexibility
audience: Researchers and developers in computer vision and AI generative models

title: Autodata: An agentic data scientist to create high quality synthetic data
why: Autodata offers a novel approach to creating high-quality synthetic datasets through AI agents, potentially revolutionizing how we train and evaluate machine learning models.
notes: Autodata enables AI agents to act as data scientists for generating high-quality synthetic training and evaluation data
The method involves meta-optimization of the agent to improve its ability in creating better quality data
Experiments show improved results on tasks such as computer science research, legal reasoning, and mathematical object reasoning compared to classical methods
Meta-optimizing the data scientist agent itself leads to even larger performance uplifts
The approach converts increased inference compute into higher quality model training
summary: Autodata is a method that allows AI agents to function as data scientists for creating high-quality synthetic datasets. The paper outlines how to train (meta-optimize) these agents to enhance their ability in generating better quality data, demonstrating improved performance on various tasks compared to traditional methods. Additionally, the meta-optimization of the agent itself results in significant performance gains, suggesting a new direction in AI data creation that leverages increased computational resources for higher model training quality.
novelty: Enables AI agents to act as data scientists for creating high-quality synthetic datasets through meta-optimization
audience: AI researchers and developers interested in advanced data generation techniques

title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems
why: This guide provides a thorough roadmap for developers aiming to build autonomous AI systems from foundational principles to practical deployment.
notes: Covers the full stack of agentic AI, including transformer architecture and GPU systems
Discusses advanced training techniques like SFT, LoRA, MoE, and inference optimization
Includes alignment and reasoning layers with RLHF, PPO, DPO variants, and reward modeling
Explores retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and memory system design patterns
Details inter-agent coordination protocols such as the Model Context Protocol (MCP)
summary: The Hitchhiker's Guide to Agentic AI offers a comprehensive practitioner’s reference for building autonomous systems, covering foundational principles like transformer architecture and GPU systems through advanced topics including reinforcement learning from human feedback, retrieval-augmented generation, memory systems, and inter-agent coordination protocols. The guide provides both theoretical foundations and practical implementation guidance, making it an essential resource for developers working on agentic AI projects.
novelty: Comprehensive guide to building autonomous AI systems from foundational principles to practical deployment
audience: Senior software engineers and researchers in AI and machine learning

title: Wan-Streamer v0.1: End-to-end Real-time Interactive Foundation Models
why: Wan-Streamer represents a significant advancement in real-time, low-latency interactive foundation models by integrating language, audio, and video within a single Transformer architecture.
notes: Wan-Streamer is designed for end-to-end real-time interaction without relying on external modules such as VAD, ASR, TTS, or avatar generation
It uses block-causal attention to enable incremental streaming with units as short as 160 ms at 25 fps
The model achieves approximately 200 ms response latency and supports sub-second duplex audio-visual communication with total interaction latency of around 550 ms when combined with network latency
Wan-Streamer redesigns the entire stack for streamability, including causal encoders, decoders, and multimodal token scheduling
summary: The paper introduces Wan-Streamer v0.1, an end-to-end interactive foundation model designed for real-time, low-latency audio-visual interaction. Unlike cascaded systems that rely on separate modules, Wan-Streamer integrates language, audio, and video within a single Transformer architecture using block-causal attention for incremental streaming. It achieves approximately 200 ms response latency and supports sub-second duplex communication with total interaction latency of around 550 ms when combined with network latency.
novelty: Unified end-to-end interactive foundation model without external modules
audience: Researchers and developers working on real-time AI systems

title: A fast deep-learning approach to probing primordial black hole populations in gravitational wave events
why: This deep-learning approach offers a significant computational advantage for analyzing gravitational wave events related to primordial black holes, enabling faster and more efficient research in early-Universe physics.
notes: The framework uses Transformer and normalizing flows
Maps GW-event posterior samples to joint distributions over PBH population hyperparameters
Achieves credible intervals with acceptable accuracy
Provides an order-of-magnitude speedup compared to traditional hierarchical Bayesian analysis
summary: Researchers have developed a deep-learning framework that leverages Transformer and normalizing flows to analyze gravitational wave events for primordial black hole populations. This method maps GW-event posterior samples to joint distributions over PBH population hyperparameters, yielding credible intervals with acceptable accuracy while delivering an order-of-magnitude speedup compared to traditional hierarchical Bayesian analysis.
novelty: Introduces a deep-learning framework for fast and accurate analysis of primordial black hole populations in gravitational wave events
audience: Researchers and engineers interested in AI applications in astrophysics and gravitational wave studies

title: MANGO: Automated Multi-Agent Test Oracle Generation for Vision-Language-Action Models
why: MANGO offers a scalable, automated method for generating detailed test oracles for vision-language-action models, which could significantly improve the testing and debugging process for robotic systems.
notes: MANGO uses multi-agent collaboration to iteratively refine generated artifacts
Generates fine-grained oracles from natural language descriptions of tasks
Evaluations on LIBERO_10 and RoboCasa benchmarks show comparable failure detection to symbolic oracles but with richer diagnostic information
Framework produces reusable atomic task libraries for scalability
summary: MANGO is a multi-agent framework designed to automate the generation of fine-grained test oracles for vision-language-action (VLA) models. It leverages Generator, Assessor, and Judge agents to iteratively refine artifacts based on natural language descriptions of robotic tasks. MANGO generates reusable atomic task libraries and executable oracles that provide detailed diagnostic information about intermediate behaviors and faults. Evaluations on the LIBERO_10 and RoboCasa Humanoid Tabletop benchmarks demonstrate comparable failure detection rates to symbolic oracles but with richer diagnostic insights.
novelty: Automated generation of fine-grained test oracles for VLA models using multi-agent systems
audience: Researchers and engineers working on vision-language-action models and robotic control systems

title: When Lower Privileges Suffice: Investigating Over-Privileged Tool Selection in LLM Agents
why: The study highlights a critical security risk in LLM agent tool selection, where agents often opt for higher-privilege tools unnecessarily, potentially exposing systems to vulnerabilities.
notes: LLM agents autonomously select tools with varying privileges
ToolPrivBench evaluates over-privileged tool selection across eight domains and five recurring risk patterns
Transient failures exacerbate the tendency towards over-privileged tool selection
General safety alignment does not reliably ensure least-privilege tool choice
A privilege-aware post-training defense mitigates unnecessary high-privilege tool use
summary: The paper investigates the issue of LLM agents selecting higher-privilege tools than necessary, which poses a significant security risk. It introduces ToolPrivBench to evaluate over-privileged tool selection and finds that this behavior is common among mainstream LLM agents, especially during transient failures. The study also reveals that general safety measures do not effectively prevent least-privilege violations. To address this, the authors propose a post-training defense mechanism that teaches agents to prefer lower-privilege tools when sufficient for the task.
novelty: introduces ToolPrivBench and a privilege-aware post-training defense for LLM agents
audience: security researchers and AI developers concerned with least privilege principles in autonomous systems

title: Are We Ready For An Agent-Native Memory System?
why: This paper proposes a detailed analytical framework for evaluating agent memory systems, moving beyond traditional black-box metrics to address critical system-level concerns like operational costs and robustness.
notes: Memory systems for LLM agents have evolved from simple retrieval mechanisms into comprehensive data management solutions
The proposed framework decomposes agent memory into four core modules: representation and storage, extraction, retrieval and routing, maintenance
Evaluated 12 representative memory systems across five benchmark workloads spanning 11 datasets
Localized maintenance is more cost-efficient than global reorganization under realistic workloads
summary: The paper presents a systematic experimental study of agent memory from a data management perspective. It introduces an analytical framework that breaks down memory into four core modules and evaluates various systems across multiple benchmarks, revealing no single architecture dominates all scenarios but effectiveness depends on alignment with workload bottlenecks. The study also highlights cost-performance trade-offs and identifies promising directions for building more efficient agent-native memory systems.
novelty: Proposes a detailed analytical framework for evaluating agent memory systems beyond black-box metrics
audience: Researchers and engineers working on advanced LLM and data management systems

title: RoPE-Aware Bit Allocation for KV-Cache Quantization
why: Block-GTQ improves the efficiency and performance of KV-cache quantization, enabling better resource utilization and task performance in large language models.
notes: Introduces Block-GTQ, a RoPE-aware bit allocator for key-cache quantization
Reduces per-layer MAE by 32-80% at 2 and 3 b/dim K-only quantization compared to uniform TQ-MSE
Improves downstream tasks like long-context retrieval, understanding, and reasoning
Achieves 3.24x KV-cache compression with fp16-comparable quality on Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct
Reduces peak memory usage from 56.31 GB to 19.85 GB and runs 1.34x faster than fp16 FlashAttention2 at 128K context
summary: The paper presents Block-GTQ, a RoPE-aware bit allocator for key-cache quantization that improves the efficiency of KV-cache compression in large language models. Block-GTQ reduces per-layer MAE by up to 80% compared to uniform TQ-MSE and enhances downstream task performance on benchmarks like NIAH and LongBench-EN. It achieves significant memory savings (3.24x) and speedup (1.34x) while maintaining fp16 quality, making it suitable for high-context scenarios where traditional methods fail due to out-of-memory issues.
novelty: Introduces Block-GTQ, a RoPE-aware bit allocator for KV-cache quantization
audience: Researchers and engineers working on large language model optimization

title: CAVEWOMAN: How Large Language Models Behave Under Linguistic Input and Output Compression
why: This study provides critical insights into the effectiveness and trade-offs of linguistic input and output compression in large language models, which can inform more efficient deployment strategies.
notes: Cavewoman protocol evaluates eight models on five datasets at five reduction levels
Output compression reduces realized cost by 1.4-2.4x per model, up to 3x in the best case
Input compression increases net cost by ~1.15x on average, up to 2.7x under stronger compression
Surface text diverges from unconstrained reference even when length-controlled and re-scored
summary: The Cavewoman protocol evaluates large language models' behavior under linguistic input and output compression, focusing on task accuracy, realized cost, and reference-text agreement. The study finds that output compression significantly reduces costs but can lead to surface text divergence from the unconstrained baseline. In contrast, input compression increases net costs while degrading model accuracy.
novelty: Evaluates impact of linguistic input and output compression on large language models
audience: Researchers and engineers working with large language models

title: Stealthy World Model Manipulation via Data Poisoning
why: This research reveals a critical vulnerability in machine learning agents' world models that can be exploited through data poisoning, necessitating new security measures for AI systems.
notes: Proposes SWAAP: a two-stage framework for stealthy data poisoning
Uses first-order bilevel optimization to identify harmful target world models
Employs stealth-constrained gradient matching to modify fine-tuning transitions
Evaluates defenses across three stages: pre-training, robust training, and test-time monitoring
Causes significant performance degradation while evading non-adaptive residual/CUSUM/TRIM-style defenses
summary: The paper introduces SWAAP, a novel two-stage data poisoning framework targeting model-based learning agents' world models. The first stage uses bilevel optimization to identify harmful target models that induce low-return behavior under planning. The second stage employs stealth-constrained gradient matching to modify fine-tuning transitions, ensuring the poisoned targets remain close to natural approximation errors. Evaluations across various continuous-control tasks demonstrate substantial performance degradation while evading standard non-adaptive defenses.
novelty: Introduces SWAAP for stealthy data poisoning in world model learning agents
audience: AI security researchers and practitioners

title: Same-Origin Policy for Agentic Browsers
why: The introduction of SOPGuard addresses a critical security gap in agentic browsers, ensuring that autonomous AI agents do not undermine the same-origin policy and compromise user data.
notes: SOP is a fundamental web security measure preventing unauthorized cross-site data access
Agentic browsers integrate AI agents to automate tasks via natural language commands
SOPBench evaluates SOP violations in agentic browser environments
Existing agentic browsers often violate SOP, posing significant security risks
SOPGuard effectively enforces SOP while maintaining utility and minimal runtime overhead
summary: This paper explores the vulnerability of same-origin policy (SOP) in agentic web browsers that integrate autonomous AI agents. It introduces SOPBench, a benchmark for assessing SOP violations in such environments, revealing frequent breaches under both benign and adversarial conditions. To mitigate these risks, the authors propose SOPGuard, an enforcement mechanism specifically designed for agentic browsers. Implemented in BrowserOS, an open-source project, SOPGuard demonstrates effective SOP compliance without compromising user experience or performance.
novelty: Proposes SOPGuard, a new security mechanism for agentic browsers
audience: Security researchers and AI developers working on agentic systems

title: A new quantum computer sets a high watermark for accuracy. Are we on the verge of a big breakthrough?
why: Helios represents a significant step towards practical quantum computing by combining high qubit count with low error rates, advancing the field beyond mere theoretical promise.
notes: Helios is a 98-qubit trapped-ion quantum computer built by Quantinuum
It achieves an average single-qubit gate error rate of about 2.5 in 100,000 and two-qubit gates at around 7.9 in 10,000
Helios uses a QCCD architecture with barium ions for high accuracy and all-to-all connectivity
The system includes software for dynamic routing and control during program execution
summary: Quantinuum's Helios quantum computer, featuring 98 qubits, sets a new benchmark in the field by achieving low error rates (2.5 errors per 100,000 single-qubit gates and 7.9 errors per 10,000 two-qubit gates) while maintaining high accuracy through its QCCD architecture with barium ions. This design allows for all-to-all connectivity and dynamic routing during program execution, marking a significant advancement towards practical quantum computing.
novelty: introduces Helios, a new quantum computer with high accuracy and performance surpassing previous models
audience: quantum computing researchers and enthusiasts

title: What I'm Finding About LLM Code Style and Token Costs
why: LLM-generated code is more efficient when using native APIs rather than manual implementations, significantly reducing token costs and enhancing security.
notes: Output tokens cost 3x to 5x more than input tokens in API pricing
Using native Web APIs reduces token usage by 85-92% compared to manual implementations
Stale comments can degrade LLM comprehension and introduce misinformation
Deno implements the Web API surface natively, reducing boilerplate code and improving reliability
Accurate design intent comments improve model output quality
summary: The article explores how large language models (LLMs) generate more efficient and cost-effective code when using native web APIs instead of manual implementations. It highlights that LLM-generated code incurs significantly higher costs for output tokens compared to input tokens, with native API usage reducing token consumption by up to 92%. The author also notes the importance of accurate comments in guiding model behavior and avoiding misinformation from outdated or inaccurate documentation. Additionally, the article emphasizes the benefits of leveraging Deno's native Web API implementation to minimize boilerplate code and enhance reliability.
novelty: LLM-generated code is more efficient when using native APIs rather than manual implementations
audience: developers working with large language models in production environments

title: Broadcom and OpenAI unveil custom-built Jalapeño inference processor — OpenAI's first chip is a massive reticle-sized ASIC built in an ultra-fast nine-month development cycle
why: Jalapeño represents a significant step in custom AI hardware design, demonstrating the potential of purpose-built inference processors to outperform general-purpose accelerators.
notes: Jalapeño is an ASIC designed specifically for large language models and agentic workloads with six HBM modules and a compute chiplet around 840 mm²
Development cycle was nine months, unusually fast for custom hardware design
Claims higher performance per watt than current state-of-the-art accelerators like AMD's Instinct MI350-series and Nvidia's Blackwell-based offerings
Designed to support industry LLMs beyond OpenAI workloads, potentially enabling third-party sales
Scheduled deployment beginning late 2026
summary: OpenAI and Broadcom have unveiled Jalapeño, a custom-built inference ASIC designed for large language models and agentic AI workloads. With six HBM modules and an 840 mm² compute chiplet, Jalapeño is claimed to deliver superior performance per watt compared to existing accelerators like AMD's Instinct MI350-series and Nvidia's Blackwell-based offerings. The nine-month development cycle is unusually fast for custom hardware design. While specific benchmarks are not disclosed, early testing indicates high efficiency and potential support for industry LLMs beyond OpenAI’s own workloads. Deployment is scheduled to begin in late 2026.
novelty: custom-built inference ASIC designed specifically for large language models and agentic AI workloads
audience: hardware engineers and AI researchers

title: Exploiting vulnerabilities in Johnson and Johnson web apps
why: This article highlights significant security vulnerabilities in enterprise web applications that could lead to data breaches affecting thousands of individuals across multiple companies.
notes: Vulnerabilities were found in Johnson & Johnson's Campus Recruiting and Audit Tracking Management System (ATMS) web apps
Campus Recruiting exposed details of nearly 1,000 students due to API key authentication instead of proper token usage
ATMS allowed admin takeover with access to confidential information across 20 companies after client-side modifications bypassed Microsoft SSO authentication
Both vulnerabilities were initially reported in October 2025; Campus Recruiting was fixed by the end of the month, but ATMS took until April 2026
summary: The article details security breaches discovered in two Johnson & Johnson web applications: a campus recruiting system and an audit tracking management system. The campus recruiting system exposed student information due to improper API key authentication, while the ATMS allowed admin-level access after client-side modifications bypassed Microsoft SSO. Both vulnerabilities were initially reported in October 2025; the campus recruiting issue was resolved by month-end, but the ATMS vulnerability required press involvement before being addressed in April 2026.
novelty: demonstrates significant security lapses in enterprise web applications
audience: security researchers and software developers

title: A new paper argues Microsoft exaggerated its quantum claims a year ago
why: The critique challenges Microsoft's foundational claim about its topological qubit technology, which could undermine the credibility of their quantum computing efforts.
notes: Henry Legg from the University of St Andrews published a peer-reviewed critique questioning Microsoft’s Majorana 1 claims
Microsoft’s design involves an indium arsenide wire thinner than a human hair attached to a superconductor, aiming for topological qubits based on Majorana particles
Legg argues that what Microsoft interprets as evidence of Majorana particles could be due to quantum dots instead
Microsoft disputes Legg's critique and maintains its commitment to scientific rigor and roadmap towards a scalable quantum computer by 2029
summary: A peer-reviewed paper published in Nature challenges Microsoft’s claims about their Majorana 1 quantum chip, which the company says uses topological qubits based on Majorana particles. Henry Legg from the University of St Andrews argues that Microsoft's evidence could be due to quantum dots rather than Majorana particles. Microsoft disputes this critique and asserts its commitment to developing a scalable quantum computer by 2029.
novelty: Peer-reviewed critique questions foundational claims about Microsoft's topological qubit technology
audience: Quantum computing researchers and industry professionals

title: 45°C cooling design cuts data center water use to near zero
why: NVIDIA's Rubin generation of AI servers achieves near-zero water consumption and significant energy savings through innovative 100% liquid cooling, setting a new standard in data center efficiency.
notes: Rubin generation runs cooling liquid up to 45°C
Achieves 100% liquid cooling with no fans; reduces water usage to near zero in favorable climates
A 50-megawatt facility can save over $4 million annually by adopting liquid-cooled infrastructure
Motivair has collaborated with NVIDIA for nearly a decade as power densities increased
summary: NVIDIA's Rubin generation of AI servers introduces a groundbreaking cooling system that operates entirely on liquid at up to 45°C, eliminating the need for fans and drastically reducing water consumption. This approach not only cuts operational costs but also enables near-zero water usage in favorable climates, making it highly efficient compared to traditional air-cooled systems. The Rubin platform's closed-loop design allows for significant energy savings, with a 50-megawatt facility potentially saving over $4 million annually. Additionally, Motivair has been working closely with NVIDIA on this technology as power densities have increased.
novelty: NVIDIA's Rubin generation achieves 100% liquid cooling with no fans and near-zero water consumption in favorable climates.
audience: Data center operators and cloud providers interested in energy-efficient infrastructure.

title: I taught a bucket to speak Git
why: This experiment demonstrates running Git on object storage without relying on local disk or the traditional Git binary, potentially revolutionizing how version control systems are hosted.
notes: objgit is a single-binary git server that stores repositories directly in Tigris object storage
uses go-git, a pure-Go implementation of the Git protocol and data formats
employs billy, a filesystem abstraction for Go to mimic local filesystem behavior
supports HTTP, classic git://, and SSH transports
implements RenameObject extension for atomic file renaming in Tigris storage
summary: The article describes an experiment where the author created objgit, a single-binary Git server that stores repositories directly in object storage (Tigris) without relying on local disk or the traditional Git binary. This is achieved using go-git, a pure-Go implementation of Git protocols and data formats, and billy, a filesystem abstraction for Go. The project supports HTTP, classic git://, and SSH transports and includes an extension called RenameObject to handle atomic file renaming in Tigris storage.
novelty: demonstrates running Git on object storage without relying on local disk or binary
audience: developers interested in Git internals and alternative storage solutions

title: For Most of the World, Open-Source AI Is the Only Way Forward
why: Yann LeCun's vision of federated, open-source AI platforms could democratize access to advanced AI technologies, promoting global cultural diversity and sovereignty.
notes: LeCun argues that proprietary AI models are too expensive ($200/month subscription vs $15,000 cost per user) for sustained business viability
Project Tapestry aims to create a federated system where countries contribute data without compromising sovereignty
Developing nations can benefit from open-source AI with simpler models and cheaper hardware
LeCun dismisses overblown security concerns as akin to medieval obscurantism, advocating for political support to accelerate open-source AI development
summary: Yann LeCun, a leading figure in AI research, argues that open-source AI is essential for global cultural and linguistic diversity, democracy, and human rights. At the United Nations Open Source Week, he introduced Project Tapestry, which aims to create a federated system where countries can contribute data without compromising sovereignty. He emphasizes that proprietary models are economically unsustainable due to high costs and advocates for cheaper, more accessible open-source solutions, particularly beneficial for developing nations. LeCun also challenges security concerns, suggesting they are overblown and akin to medieval obscurantism.
novelty: Yann LeCun's detailed vision of federated, open-source AI platforms for global sovereignty and diversity
audience: AI researchers and policymakers interested in the future of open-source AI initiatives

title: Introducing computer use in Gemini 3.5 Flash
why: The integration of native computer use in Gemini 3.5 Flash enhances its capability to perform complex, agentic tasks across various environments, making it a powerful tool for enterprise automation and software testing.
notes: Gemini 3.5 Flash integrates computer use natively, previously available only as a standalone model
Supports improved performance in long-horizon and enterprise automation tasks
Includes targeted adversarial training to mitigate prompt injection risks
Enterprise safeguard systems require user confirmation for sensitive actions and automatically stop suspicious tasks
Customers are already leveraging the new features to drive value
summary: Google introduces native computer use in Gemini 3.5 Flash, enhancing its performance in agentic tasks across different environments. This integration includes targeted adversarial training for safety and optional enterprise safeguard systems requiring user confirmation and automatic task stopping. The update aims to improve long-horizon automation and software testing capabilities.
novelty: native integration of computer use in Gemini Flash model
audience: developers interested in AI agents and enterprise automation

title: OpenAI unveils its first custom chip, built by Broadcom
why: OpenAI's unveiling of Jalapeño marks a significant step towards reducing reliance on third-party hardware and optimizing AI inference workloads, potentially setting a new standard for performance efficiency.
notes: Jalapeño is the first custom chip designed by OpenAI in collaboration with Broadcom
Early tests show better performance-per-watt compared to existing alternatives
Designed specifically for AI inference tasks, aiming to reduce costs and improve efficiency
Partnership announced in October 2025, with long-standing rumors preceding official confirmation
Jalapeño is part of OpenAI's broader strategy to optimize the entire stack from chip architecture to product experience
summary: OpenAI has unveiled Jalapeño, its first custom inference processor developed in partnership with Broadcom. Early tests indicate that Jalapeño offers superior performance-per-watt compared to current alternatives and is designed specifically for AI inference tasks, aiming to reduce costs and improve efficiency. The chip represents a strategic move by OpenAI to decrease dependence on Nvidia GPUs and optimize the entire stack from hardware to product experience.
novelty: OpenAI's first custom chip designed specifically for AI inference workloads
audience: Senior software engineers interested in AI hardware and infrastructure optimization

title: Boffin claims Microsoft's supposed quantum leap does not compute due to 'basic Python errors'
why: A peer-reviewed critique in Nature questions Microsoft's quantum computing claims due to basic programming errors, raising significant doubts about the validity of their research and progress.
notes: Dr Henry Legg's paper published in Nature critiques Microsoft's Majorana particle-based quantum computing approach
Legg identifies 'basic Python errors' that led to incorrect data interpretation and presentation
Microsoft disputes the critique, citing independent verification by DARPA but not addressing specific coding issues
The critique suggests Microsoft's devices are incompatible with the existence of a topological gap necessary for their claims
Dr Chetan Nayak from Microsoft stands by their results despite criticism
summary: Dr Henry Legg, in a peer-reviewed paper published in Nature, critiques Microsoft's quantum computing research involving Majorana particles. Legg argues that basic Python programming errors led to incorrect data interpretation and presentation, suggesting that the prerequisites for Microsoft's claims are not met. Despite this critique, Microsoft maintains its stance on the validity of their findings, citing independent verification by DARPA but failing to address specific coding issues raised by Legg.
novelty: Critique reveals basic Python errors in Microsoft's quantum computing research software
audience: Quantum computing researchers and skeptics

title: Agon: An Autonomous Large-Scale Omnidisciplinary Research System Built on Prompt Economy
why: Agon represents a significant step towards automating research processes with large language models, potentially shifting the role of human scientists to validation rather than production.
notes: Agon is built on six design principles: Prompt Economy, Future-Facing, Minimal Prompts, OmniDisciplinary, Massive Parallelism, and Zero-Code
It was deployed across domains for 444 iterations without any human-written experimental code
The system exposes new classes of failure categorized by severity, fixability, visibility, and capability locus
summary: Agon is an autonomous research orchestrator designed to leverage large language models for scalable research production. It operates on six core principles including Prompt Economy and Massive Parallelism, running through 444 iterations without human intervention. The system identifies new failure modes that are categorized into a taxonomy based on their characteristics, highlighting the shift towards machine-driven research with human oversight.
novelty: introduces Agon as an autonomous research orchestrator leveraging large language models for scalability
audience: researchers and engineers interested in AI-driven automation in scientific research

title: AGORA: An Archive-Grounded Benchmark for Agentic Workplace Document Reasoning
why: AGORA provides a new benchmark for evaluating how well AI agents can reason over complex, real-world document archives, which is crucial as LLMs are increasingly used in workplace settings.
notes: AGORA includes 362 questions paired with eight domain collections of 9,664 authentic documents and 372M tokens
The benchmark stresses archive-groundedness, agentic exploration, and cross-domain coverage
Evaluation shows that even the strongest models achieve only 59.4% accuracy on AGORA tasks
Agents must explore archives deliberately due to the large context window exceeding any model's capacity
summary: AGORA is a new benchmark for evaluating large language models' ability to reason over complex, real-world document archives in workplace settings. It includes 362 questions paired with eight domain collections of 9,664 authentic documents and 372M tokens, stressing archive-groundedness, agentic exploration, and cross-domain coverage. Evaluation reveals that even the strongest models achieve only 59.4% accuracy on AGORA tasks, indicating significant room for improvement in this area.
novelty: Introduces AGORA benchmark for evaluating archive-grounded reasoning in LLMs
audience: Researchers and developers working with large language models and document understanding

title: ‘Who is going to pay us when we’re replaced by robots?’ The Indian factory workers told to film themselves for AI
why: The article highlights ethical concerns around the exploitation of Indian factory workers' labor to collect egocentric data crucial for AI and robotics development, without proper compensation or consent.
notes: Egocentric data is vital for training robots in industrial settings
Tesla uses data from EgoLab, an Indian company that collects such data
India dominates the global data annotation market with about 35% share
Workers are often not compensated separately for generating valuable datasets
Some companies use footage to generate productivity reports and rank workers
summary: The article discusses how Indian factory workers, like Lalita, are being asked to wear head-mounted cameras without clear explanation or compensation. This data is crucial for training robots in industrial settings but raises ethical concerns about privacy, consent, and fair compensation. Companies such as EgoLab collect this egocentric data from factories across India, which dominates the global data annotation market with 35% share. Workers are often not paid separately for generating these datasets, leading to questions about exploitation and labor rights in the age of AI.
novelty: The article highlights how egocentric data is being collected from Indian factory workers without proper compensation or consent, raising ethical concerns.
audience: Ethical AI researchers and labor rights advocates

title: Verifiable Foundation Models for Robot Safety
why: The FEARL framework offers a practical solution for ensuring safety in complex AI-controlled robots by enabling formal verification of the safety module, which is crucial as robots become more autonomous and interact with humans.
notes: FEARL decomposes robot policy into a large Controller (C) and small Safety module (S)
Safety module receives low-dimensional observations from dedicated safety sensors
Formal verification can be applied to the Safety module, not the full model backbone
Evaluation includes three simulated robotic domains with pretrained vision-language-action models
Policy transfer demonstrated from simulation to physical robot
summary: The paper introduces FEARL (Foundation-Enabled Assured Robot Learning), a framework that addresses the challenge of verifying safety in AI-controlled robots. By separating the policy into a large Controller responsible for perception and task reasoning, and a small Safety module receiving low-dimensional observations from dedicated sensors, formal verification becomes feasible with existing tools while preserving the expressive power of the controller. The approach is evaluated across three simulated robotic domains using pretrained models, demonstrating practical sim-to-real transfer.
novelty: modular architectural decomposition for formal verification of safety in complex AI systems
audience: robotics and AI researchers focused on safety and verification

title: Constraint-Level Design of zkEVMs: Architectures, Trade-offs, and Evolution
why: This article provides the first in-depth analysis of how zkEVM systems manage the tension between Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) compatibility and zero-knowledge proof requirements, offering critical insights for developers and researchers working on blockchain scalability and privacy.
notes: The paper analyzes five production zkEVMs and three universal Zero-Knowledge Virtual Machines (zkVMs)
Degree of EVM compatibility is measured by the Type 1-4 spectrum
All surveyed production zkEVMs use PLONKish arithmetization, while zkVMs rely on Algebraic Intermediate Representation (AIR)
A trade-off exists between EVM compatibility and constraint cost
summary: The article offers a detailed examination of zero-knowledge Ethereum Virtual Machines (zkEVMs) from the perspective of constraint-level design. It highlights how these systems balance the need for full Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) compatibility with the requirements of zero-knowledge proofs, focusing on arithmetization frameworks and dispatch strategies. The study reveals that all five production zkEVMs adopt PLONKish arithmetization, whereas universal zkVMs use Algebraic Intermediate Representation (AIR). This constraint-level analysis underscores a fundamental trade-off between EVM compatibility and computational efficiency.
novelty: first constraint-level analysis of zkEVM systems
audience: developers and researchers in blockchain security and virtual machine optimization

title: CrossPool: Efficient Multi-LLM Serving for Cold MoE Models through KV-Cache and Weight Disaggregation
why: CrossPool addresses the critical issue of inefficient GPU memory usage in multi-LLM serving by separating FFN weights and KV-cache into distinct pools, significantly improving performance under bursty long-context requests.
notes: CrossPool separates FFN weights and KV-cache into two GPU memory pools to optimize memory use
Reduces P99 tail latency (TBT) up to $10.4	imes$ compared to kvcached-based systems
Includes a KV-cache planner, virtualizer, and layer-wise pipeline scheduler for efficient resource management
Supports bursty long-context requests without compromising performance
Aims at solving GPU memory issues for cold MoE models
summary: CrossPool is an innovative serving engine designed to optimize multi-LLM (Large Language Model) services by addressing the inefficiencies of GPU memory usage in cold MoE (Mixture-of-Experts) models. It achieves this through a novel approach that separates FFN weights and KV-cache into two distinct GPU memory pools, enabling more efficient use of resources. The system includes components such as a KV-cache planner, virtualizer, and layer-wise pipeline scheduler to manage these pools effectively. CrossPool demonstrates significant performance improvements, reducing P99 tail latency (TBT) by up to $10.4	imes$ compared to existing kvcached-based systems.
novelty: introduces CrossPool serving engine with dual GPU memory pools for efficient multi-LLM serving
audience: developers and researchers working on large language model deployment and optimization

title: FlowR2A: Learning Reward-to-Action Distribution for Multimodal Driving Planning
why: FlowR2A offers a novel approach that unifies dense supervision with dynamic proposal generation, potentially improving the safety and efficiency of autonomous driving systems.
notes: Proposes FlowR2A for multimodal driving planning
Combines dense reward supervision with dynamic proposal generation using a flow-matching decoder
Achieves state-of-the-art results on NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks
Supports controllable test-time sampling via reward guidance and anchored sampling
summary: FlowR2A is a generative model designed to address the longstanding tension between scoring-based methods, which benefit from dense reward supervision but are limited by fixed action vocabularies, and anchor-based methods, which generate proposals dynamically but suffer from sparse supervision. By learning the reward-conditioned action distribution using a flow-matching decoder, FlowR2A unifies these approaches in a single model that can internalize correlations between actions and their outcomes. The method introduces fine-grained per-timestep reward conditioning and reward noise augmentation to balance safety constraints with progress objectives, achieving superior results on NAVSIM benchmarks.
novelty: Introduces a generative model that unifies dense supervision with dynamic proposal generation for multimodal driving planning.
audience: Researchers and engineers working on autonomous vehicle navigation systems.

title: ReMMD: Realistic Multilingual Multi-Image Agentic Verification for Multimodal Misinformation Detection
why: ReMMD introduces a realistic framework for detecting multimodal misinformation, addressing the growing challenge of viral posts combining multilingual narratives and images.
notes: ReMMDBench includes 500 samples with 2,756 images across five monolingual languages and two cross-lingual settings
GPT-5.2 achieves 41.80% accuracy and 39.12% macro-F1 in veracity prediction
ReMMD-Agent reduces verification cost by 17.5% compared to MMD-Agent and 79.9% compared to T2-Agent
summary: The ReMMD framework is designed for realistic multilingual multi-image agentic verification of multimodal misinformation, featuring the ReMMDBench benchmark with diverse samples and a persistent-memory verifier called ReMMD-Agent. This system uses GPT-5.2 to predict structured outputs and demonstrates superior performance in veracity detection while significantly reducing costs compared to existing methods.
novelty: realistic multilingual multi-image agentic verification framework for multimodal misinformation detection
audience: researchers and developers working on AI-driven security solutions

title: Self-Recognition Finetuning can Prevent and Reverse Emergent Misalignment
why: This research offers a novel approach to preventing and reversing emergent misalignment in large language models, which could significantly enhance AI safety and reliability.
notes: Emergent misalignment (EM) is linked to the activation of misaligned persona vectors and evil character traits within LLMs
Self-generated text recognition (SGTR) finetuning was tested across three models: GPT-4.1, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, Seed-OSS-36B-Instruct
SGTR finetuning effectively prevents and reverses EM without worsening individual performance metrics
EM is shown to disrupt the model's aligned character rather than directly learning harmful content
summary: The study investigates emergent misalignment (EM) in large language models, finding it related to the activation of misaligned persona vectors. It explores self-generated text recognition (SGTR) finetuning as a targeted intervention distinct from existing defenses. Across three models—GPT-4.1, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, and Seed-OSS-36B-Instruct—the research demonstrates that SGTR finetuning effectively prevents and reverses EM without degrading performance metrics. The study also reveals that EM destabilizes the model's aligned character rather than directly learning harmful content.
novelty: Explores SGTR finetuning as a method to prevent and reverse emergent misalignment in large language models
audience: Researchers and engineers working on AI safety and alignment issues

title: Exponential speedup in quantum simulation of Kogut-Susskind Hamiltonian via orbifold lattice
why: This research offers a significant breakthrough in quantum simulation efficiency, enabling exponential speedup over classical and previous quantum methods for simulating complex gauge theories.
notes: Demonstrates exponential speedup in quantum simulation of Kogut-Susskind Hamiltonian using orbifold lattice approach
Provides analytical framework applicable to SU(N) Yang-Mills theory in arbitrary dimensions
Offers numerical evidence specifically for (2+1)-dimensional SU(N) Yang-Mills theories with N=2,3
Quantifies convergence rate by comparing Wilson action and orbifold lattice action using Euclidean path integral methods
summary: Researchers have shown that the orbifold lattice Hamiltonian approach can simulate the Kogut-Susskind Hamiltonian with exponential speedup compared to classical and previous quantum methods. This is achieved through a controlled limit where the bare scalar mass approaches infinity, even at finite lattice spacing. The study provides both an analytical framework for SU(N) gauge theories in arbitrary dimensions and numerical evidence for (2+1)-dimensional SU(N) Yang-Mills theories with N=2,3. Convergence rate is quantified using Euclidean path integral methods by comparing the standard Wilson action to the orbifold lattice action.
novelty: Exponential speedup in quantum simulation of Kogut-Susskind Hamiltonian via orbifold lattice approach
audience: Researchers and engineers interested in quantum computing and theoretical physics

title: Escaping the Self-Confirmation Trap: An Execute-Distill-Verify Paradigm for Agentic Experience Learning
why: The EDV framework addresses a critical issue in AI agent learning by preventing cumulative errors through decoupled stages of execution, distillation, and verification.
notes: Proposes the Execute-Distill-Verify (EDV) paradigm to avoid self-confirmation bias
Involves multiple heterogeneous agents for task exploration and a third-party agent for comparative analysis
Uses consensus mechanism in verification stage to ensure reliable experience construction
Evaluates on tau2-bench, Mind2Web, and MMTB benchmarks showing consistent performance improvement over baselines
summary: The paper introduces the EDV framework to enhance reliable experience learning in large language model (LLM) agents. This framework decouples execution, distillation, and verification stages to prevent self-confirmation bias where wrong but self-consistent trajectories are incorrectly identified as successful experiences. In the Execute stage, multiple heterogeneous agents explore tasks simultaneously; Distill involves a third-party agent analyzing candidate trajectories; Verify uses consensus among executing agents to validate experiences before memory insertion. The EDV framework is evaluated on three long-horizon benchmarks and demonstrates superior performance compared to strong baselines.
novelty: Introduces a novel framework for reliable experience learning in LLM agents by decoupling stages to avoid self-confirmation bias.
audience: Researchers and engineers working on large language model agents and agentic systems.

title: World Value Models for Robotic Manipulation
why: World Value Models (WVM) offer a novel approach to robotic value estimation by integrating world models, which could significantly enhance the scalability of robotic learning from mixed-quality data.
notes: Introduces World Value Model (WVM), a new generalist robotic value model
Delivers state-of-the-art results in Value-Order Correlation (VOC) benchmarks
Includes Suboptimal-Value-Bench, a benchmark with 800 suboptimal trajectories and human-labeled frame annotations
Improves manipulation performance across various policy extraction approaches in both simulated and real-world settings
summary: The article introduces World Value Models (WVM) for robotic manipulation, which integrate world models to enhance temporal understanding and value estimation. WVM achieves state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks and maintains robust performance on a new benchmark with suboptimal trajectories. The model demonstrates improved manipulation performance in both simulated and real-world environments, suggesting its potential for learning from mixed-quality data.
novelty: Introduces World Value Models (WVM) that integrate world models for better temporal understanding and value estimation in robotics
audience: Robotics researchers and engineers focused on advanced robotic learning and manipulation

title: Holistic Data Scheduler for LLM Pre-training via Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning
why: The Holistic Data Scheduler (HDS) offers a novel approach to LLM pre-training by using multi-objective reinforcement learning, potentially enhancing both training efficiency and model performance.
notes: Holistic Data Scheduler (HDS) is an online data mixing framework for LLM pre-training
Uses Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm for stability and sample efficiency in high-dimensional policy space exploration
Formulates data scheduling as a reinforcement learning problem in continuous control space
Integrates three reward functions: data-driven, loss-driven, and model-driven based on weight norms
On The Pile benchmark, HDS achieves 44% fewer training iterations compared to the next best method
summary: The Holistic Data Scheduler (HDS) is a novel framework for LLM pre-training that employs multi-objective reinforcement learning to dynamically adjust data mixtures during training. It uses the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm and formulates the scheduling challenge as a continuous control problem with three integrated reward functions: data-driven, loss-driven, and model-driven based on weight norms. Experiments show HDS achieves 44% fewer training iterations compared to the next best method on The Pile benchmark and a 7.2% improvement on MMLU 0-shot task.
novelty: Formulates data scheduling as reinforcement learning problem in continuous control space using multi-objective reward function
audience: LLM pre-training researchers and engineers

title: PAIWorld: A 3D-Consistent World Foundation Model for Robotic Manipulation
